chunks.text: Katherine A. Yelick is a Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at UC Berkeley since 1991, with a joint research appointment at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory since 1996, where she serves as Associate Laboratory Director for Computing Sciences. Her research focuses on parallel programming languages, automatic performance tuning, performance analysis, parallel algorithms, and optimizing compilers. She leads the Berkeley Unified Parallel C (UPC) project and co-leads the Berkeley Benchmarking and Optimization (Bebop) group. As ALD for Computing Sciences, she oversees the National Energy Research Scientific Computing Center (NERSC), the Energy Sciences Network (ESnet), and the Computational Research Division (CRD).

Yelick earned her Ph.D. in EECS from MIT in 1991, a S.M. in EECS from MIT in 1985, and a B.S. in EECS from MIT in 1982. Her research areas include Programming Systems (PS), Scientific Computing (SCI), and Biosystems & Computational Biology (BIO), with a focus on parallel programming techniques. She is affiliated with research centers such as the Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing (SITC) and SpeciaLIzed Computing Ecosystems (SLICE).

Selected publications include works on extreme scale de novo metagenome assembly (SC'18), communication-avoiding optimization methods for distributed massive-scale sparse inverse covariance estimation (AISTATS 2018), merBench: PGAS benchmarks for high performance genome assembly (2017), and a whole-genome shotgun approach for assembling the hexaploid bread wheat genome (Genome Biology 2015). Other notable publications cover communication-optimal N-body algorithms, communication avoiding and overlapping for numerical linear algebra, and the Titanium experience in parallel languages and compilers.

Honors and awards include the CRA Distinguished Service Award (2022), 2020 Berkeley Lab Citation, HPCwire Outstanding Leadership in HPC (Editor's Choice, 2019), AAAS Fellow (2018), American Academy of Arts and Sciences Member (2017), National Academy of Engineering Member (2017), ACM-IEEE CS Ken Kennedy Award (2015), Athena Lecturer Award (2013), ACM Fellow (2012), Okawa Research Grant (2005), and Diane S. McEntyre Award for Excellence in Teaching Computer Science (2001).
